comments.text: I've got GPT-5.5 Pro to prove a stronger result on odd-cycle transversals and then the construction is based on generalized Mycielski graphs. Here's the note that settles the problem.

I am concerned that your note is formatted as a traditional paper, with yourself as sole author, without mentioning GPT at all, while this comment (and your general apparent process) suggests the proof was entirely generated by GPT.
Sure, this was pretty automatic on my side - I've changed the author in the note to me+GPT, but happy to change to something else too. I'm all in for proper attributions.

It of course depends on what you plan on doing with the note, but in general, especially when arguments are almost entirely generated by AI, this should be clearly indicated (either by authorship or explicit acknowledgement). You have been doing this on your comments here, but it should also be done on the PDFs, which may be shared separately to this forum.
Of course, I fully agree. These notes are mostly not arxiv/journal ready, and in any submission or posting elsewhere I mention AI use. I'll make sure it's also visible clearly in these notes.
These writeups do sometimes surface explicitly in searches (for example GPT sometimes locates them), so I think AI disclosure should be explicit within the writeups.

Note the standard is not to add as authorship, but in later acknowledgement at the end of the paper. Adding prompts/SKILLS.md used in an appendix can be very helpful to people designing agentic flows. There are significant and important contributions that are being made here, but only if shared. The domain is https://www.ulam.ai/, so no real worries, but would love to know how you're doing it so quickly! Maybe a new paper you might reference for agentic techniques would be great and cite that in acknowledgements.
Congrats! Standard check found no issues.

Next to Nat's AI standard check, hereby also confirmed human verification/ review. Overall, once the proof is fully understood, it is again a rather elegant solution :) Minor comments: *abstract was longer than one usually would like to be *the last part of the intro felt technical/ difficult, but is clear once one read the whole paper *Theorem 2.3 by Stiebitz: the reference is not added when applied (while there are two references by Stiebitz), so one has to remember from the introduction (most people do not read linearly) *fourth line proof Theorem 4.1: the result $\textbf{is}$ known One added remark; In the finite case, the iterative procedure has to be redone everytime for higher chromatic number. The simple solution for the infinite graph was to take the disjoint union of all of them. By adding one edge between consecutive graphs, one can also obtain an infinite connected graph.

A Lean 4 / Mathlib formalization is here: Proof.lean. Type-check it in-browser here: live.lean-lang.org. Depends on Stiebitz's theorem (chromatic number of recursively-built generalized Mycielski graphs) as an axiom. Using Claude Code 4.7 & GPT-5.5 Pro, checked in Lean 4.27 and 4.28 and SafeVerify. I've also taken tried to take the FC form and prove equivalence with it.
Thanks! Sorry, I have just seen this. By discussion with GPT-5.5 Thinking, I confirm this is a valid Lean proof following Chojecki's informal proof. Therefore, this problem has been formalized.
A Positive Answer to [74] Would Imply a Positive Answer to [750]. See proof here
Would this work in reverse? Such as a positive answer to 750 leading us to a positive answer to 74? Is there roughly any shared machinery you can use between the two problems?
I tried getting some results on [74] but couldn't.
Understandable, it looks to be quite difficult. Would you say the main blocker of 74 is lack of machinery or simply finding the correct machinery, if that makes sense.

I'd say the blocker is more "finding the correct machinery" than lack of machinery altogether. The implication \(74 \Rightarrow 750\) is quite natural because edge-bipartisation gives a large independent set. If \(\beta(H)\) is the minimum number of edges to delete to make \(H\) bipartite, then\[ \alpha(H)\ge \frac{|V(H)|-\beta(H)}2. \]Indeed, take a minimum bipartising edge set, choose one endpoint from each deleted edge, remove those vertices, and then take the larger side of the remaining bipartite graph. So a positive answer to [74] gives [750] after replacing \(g\) by roughly \(2f\). But the reverse direction does not follow from this machinery. A positive answer to [750] only gives large independent sets, or in the stronger Mycielski-style proof, small vertex odd-cycle transversals:\[ \operatorname{oct}(H)=\min\{|T|:H-T\text{ is bipartite}\}. \]That is much weaker than bounding \(\beta(H)\), the number of edges needed to make \(H\) bipartite. The obstruction is especially visible in generalized Mycielski graphs. For the vertex version, the apex is cheap: if an odd cycle goes through the apex, you can delete the apex as one vertex. This is why one gets estimates of the form\[ \operatorname{oct}(M_s(H)[X]) \le s\,\operatorname{oct}(H[P]) + 1. \]That \(+1\) is harmless for [750]. For [74], however, you cannot delete the apex. You have to delete enough edges from the apex-created odd cycles to destroy all of them, and that can be huge. For example, take \(H=tK_2\), a matching of size \(t\). Then \(H\) is bipartite, so\[ \operatorname{oct}(H)=0,\qquad \beta(H)=0. \]But in the one-step Mycielski/cone construction, the apex creates \(t\) edge-disjoint odd cycles sharing that apex. Deleting the apex kills all of them, so\[ \operatorname{oct}(M_1(H))=1, \]whereas edge-bipartisation must hit each of the \(t\) odd cycles separately, giving\[ \beta(M_1(H))=t. \]So the Mycielski/cone machinery that works for [750] does not directly work for [74]. So I don't see any way [750] can be reversed into [74] by the same argument. There is shared machinery but for [74] one would need an edge-profile version of that machinery, controlling not just whether odd cycles can be killed by a few vertices, but how many edge deletions the apex-created odd cycles force. I, with the help of ChatGPT, also tried looking at a possible colour-forcing/sender route. The idea would be to build bounded-\(\beta\), high-oddgirth gadgets forcing two nonadjacent terminals to receive different colours. But this seems to run into a major issue: for fixed \(K\), \(\beta(S)\le K\) and odd-girth at least \(3K+5\) appear to imply that any two nonadjacent vertices can still be equal-coloured in some proper 3-colouring. So pairwise sender-type gadgets do not seem to be the right way to attack [74] either. A longer, mostly AI-generated, proof on that sender obstruction is available here: No bounded-edge-bipartisation high-odd-girth three-colour inequality senders (via archive.org). To be clear: this note is almost fully AI-generated and has not been extensively checked, so it should not be taken at face value as a finished proof. I have not been able to find any issues with the proof myself, though that doesn't make it gospel. I mention it only as a reference for the obstruction I have in mind. So my current guess is: the main missing thing missing is an edge-bipartisation analogue of the Mycielski local profile theorem. Either such an edge-profile theorem exists, which would be a plausible route to [74], or the obstruction caused by the apex edge-cost where a counterexample to that route could appear.

The correct reference for the $f(m)=\epsilon m$ result seems to be Theorem 1 in (P. Erdős, A. Hajnal and E. Szemerédi, "On almost bipartite large chromatic graphs", Ann. Discrete Math. 12 (1982), 117–123). The stated theorem says you can find a bipartite induced subgraph with at least $(1-\epsilon) n$ vertices. Taking the larger half of the bipartition then confirms the conjecture/claim by [Er69b]/[Er94b]. (The reference was located by GPT-5, who also noticed the small deduction above by itself in the process, to my knowledge.) (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
